introduction: Asphyxia in neonates can lead to hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy, affecting various bodily functions depending on severity.

features: Global hypertonia with brisk deep tendon reflexes

non_drug: Serial monitoring of parameters (oxygen saturation via pulse oximetry, random blood glucose, serum electrolytes, urea and creatinine, serum calcium, serum troponin, serum aspartate aminotransferase, serum alanine aminotransferase where available)
Apnea management with frequent airway clearing and ventilation by bag and mask
Nil per oral until peristalsis is present and respiratory rate is normal, feeding with expressed breast milk via nasogastric or orogastric tube
Monitor urine output, weigh diaper or catheterize for adequate monitoring, and consult nephrologist if oliguria noted
drug: Use of inotropes like dobutamine or dopamine for hypotension not corrected by fluid bolus
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Prophylactic phenobarbitone is no longer standard practice
Fluid restriction in perinatal asphyxia management is no longer favored
supportive_measures: Derangements corrected according to standard protocols
Prevent over-hydration, administer exact daily maintenance fluid requirement
Use fluid bolus of normal saline in aliquots if circulatory collapse occurs
Treat hypoglycemia as per serial serum monitoring

prognosis: Persistence of abnormal cry and motor functions beyond first week suggests severe cerebral damage
